title: Closure of Orbits of the Pure Mapping Class Group on the Character Variety
speaker: Alireza Salehi Golsefidy
abstract:
For every surface S, the pure mapping class group G_S acts on the (SL_2)-character variety Ch_S of a fundamental group P of S. The character variety Ch_S is a scheme over the ring of integers. Classically this action on the real points Ch_S(R) of the character variety has been studied in the context of the Teichmuller theory and SL(2,R)-representations of P. In a seminal work, Goldman studied this action on a subset of Ch_S(R) which comes from SU(2)-representations of P. In this case, Goldman showed that if S is of genus g greater than 1 and zero punctures, then the action of G_S is ergodic. Previte and Xia studied this question from topological point of view, and when g greater than 0, proved that the orbit closure is as large as algebraically possible. Bourgain, Gamburd, and Sarnak studied this action on the F_p-points Ch_S(F_p) of the character variety where S is a puncture torus. They conjectured that in this case, this action has only two orbits, where one of the orbits has only one point. Recently, this conjecture was proved for large enough primes by Chen. When S is an n-punture sphere, the finite orbits of this action on Ch_S(C) are connected to the algebraic solutions of Painleve differential equations. In this talk, I will report on our recent contributions to this theory. Here are some sample results:
An almost complete description of the Zariski-closure of infinite G_S-orbits in Ch_S(F) where F is a characteristic zero field. Answering a question of Goldman-Previte-Xia by understanding the orbit closure of G_S on SU(2)-representation part of Ch_S(R) where S is an n-puncture sphere. Show that the original result of Previte and Xia is not accurate and give a description of the cases where it fails. Proving that in most cases the closure of G_S-orbits in the p-adic integer points Ch_S(Z_p) are open within given polynomial constrains. We give precise description of exceptional cases. (This is a joint work with Natallie Tamam.)
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Surface group presentation and optimized generators
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the punctured surface Sigma_{g,n}, gives a presentation of its fundamental group with generators a_i,b_i,c_j and one boundary relation, and chooses orientations so ordered generator words correspond to simple curves.

proof_stages.label: SL2 character variety via trace algebra
proof_stages.summary: Representations of the surface group into SL2 are organized as a representation scheme, then traces and the Cayley-Hamilton/Fricke relations are used to define the finitely generated coordinate ring of the SL2 character variety and the natural map from representations to characters.

proof_stages.label: Absolutely irreducible locus and quotient/lifting properties
proof_stages.summary: The speaker removes the reducible locus using commutator discriminants, describes when two absolutely irreducible representations with the same character are conjugate, and explains lifting/surjectivity results over compact DVRs and their finite quotients.

proof_stages.label: Pure mapping class group and induced algebraic action
proof_stages.summary: After defining the pure mapping class group, Dehn twists, and generators, the Dehn-Nielsen identification is used to realize it through boundary-preserving outer automorphisms, which act by precomposition on representation schemes and by trace pullback on character varieties.

proof_stages.label: Invariant strata and modified relative character varieties
proof_stages.summary: The possible orbit closures are constrained by invariants such as absolute irreducibility, sufficient Zariski density, boundary traces, and whether boundary monodromies are central, leading to the modified relative character variety as the expected closure locus.

proof_stages.label: Characteristic-zero Zariski and Archimedean density theorems
proof_stages.summary: The main density theorem is stated: outside two explicit exceptional families, an infinite orbit of a sufficiently Zariski-dense point over a characteristic-zero field is dense in the modified relative character variety, with an analogous SU2 dense-image statement in the real topology.

proof_stages.label: p-adic openness and finite-level stability
proof_stages.summary: The p-adic version asserts that, under the same qualitative hypotheses and dense image, orbit closures are open in the expected p-adic relative locus, and a mod p^k consequence gives only finitely many closure types and stability of orbit counts under a boundary nondegeneracy condition.

examples.name: Pair-of-pants character variety
examples.note: The case g=0,n=3 is used to make the trace-coordinate construction concrete: the SL2 character variety is an affine 3-space, matching the three boundary lengths in Teichmuller theory.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes to g=0,n=3, identifies the generators/traces, notes there is no relation, and derives that the pair-of-pants character variety is affine 3-space.

passes.reason: Minimal pair-of-pants case with no internal relative moduli and full character variety just affine 3-space, used illustratively.
The pair-of-pants case is a minimal free-rank-two surface case whose SL2 character variety is just A^3 with no internal moduli, used as a base calculation.
The pair-of-pants case is the complexity-zero base surface, with a relation-free character variety A^3 rather than a typical higher trace variety.
The pair-of-pants case is the minimal low-complexity surface case, and its character variety collapses to the relation-free affine space A^3; it is used illustratively, not to refute a claim.
The pair-of-pants/F2 case is a minimal low-complexity character variety with no trace relation, namely the homogeneous affine space A^3.
The pair of pants is the minimal-complexity surface, with absolute SL2 character variety just A^3 and no internal moduli.
Used only as a first computation; the pair of pants is a minimal base surface and its SL2 character variety has the especially collapsed/no-relation form A^3.
Minimal pants case with free-rank-two trace algebra A^3 and no trace relation, used as the simplest illustrative character variety.
Used as a basic illustrative case, and the pair-of-pants character variety is a minimal no-relation affine base case.
It is used as the minimal pair-of-pants/base case where the character variety is simply A^3 determined by boundary traces.
the three-punctured sphere is the minimal pair-of-pants case and its character variety is the relation-free affine space A^3, used only as an illustration.
the pants character variety is a complexity-zero/no-internal-moduli base case used only to illustrate the affine A^3 computation

examples.name: Once-punctured torus as affine 3-space
examples.note: For g=1,n=1, traces of a, b, and ab freely generate the character variety, giving another low-complexity affine 3-space example.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes to the once-punctured torus, writes its generators and relation, identifies the three trace coordinates, and concludes there are no further relations so it is affine 3-space.

passes.reason: Low-complexity rank-two free-group case with no Fricke relation, so the character variety is affine 3-space rather than a typical higher-complexity one.
The once-punctured torus full character variety is again the rank-two free-group base case A^3 with no trace relation, not used to refute a claim.
The once-punctured torus is a lowest-rank free-group surface case whose SL2 trace algebra is just polynomial A^3 with no relation.
The once-punctured torus is another minimal rank-two/free-group case whose SL2 character variety is just A^3 with no further relation; it is a basic comparison example rather than an adversarial one.
The once-punctured torus is another rank-two minimal case whose full SL2 character variety is relation-free affine 3-space.
The once-punctured torus gives the rank-two free-group base case, so its absolute SL2 character variety is A^3 with no trace relation.
Used as another illustrative low-complexity computation; the once-punctured torus is the minimal positive-genus free-rank-two case whose character variety is the homogeneous affine space A^3.
Low-complexity once-punctured torus again has the relation-free SL2 character variety A^3, serving as an illustration rather than a refutation.
Used as another simple illustration, and the once-punctured torus character variety being affine 3-space is a low-complexity no-relation case.
It is presented as another rank-two minimal free-group case whose SL2 character variety is affine 3-space with no relation.
the once-punctured torus gives the rank-two free-group character variety A^3, a highly regular low-complexity case used as another illustration.
the once-punctured-torus case is the low-rank F2 case with no trace relation, so the full character variety is affine A^3

examples.name: Four-punctured sphere Fricke surface
examples.note: The g=0,n=4 case illustrates that character varieties need not be affine space: after fixing boundary traces, the remaining trace coordinates satisfy a quadratic Fricke-type relation.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker works through the four-punctured sphere case by choosing trace variables x,y,z and boundary trace parameters and describing the resulting Fricke-type quadratic relation.

passes.reason: The four-punctured-sphere Fricke surface is used to show the preceding affine-space pattern fails once a nontrivial relation appears.
The four-punctured-sphere Fricke cubic is a standard relative character surface, introduced to show the preceding affine-space pattern fails.
The four-punctured-sphere Fricke surface is used to show the preceding affine-space behavior does not persist; structurally it is a standard nondegenerate cubic family member.

The four-punctured sphere is the first nontrivial sphere case giving a Fricke relation rather than affine space, and it is explicitly used to warn that the preceding affine examples do not generalize.

examples.name: Teichmuller space inside the SL2(R) character variety
examples.note: In response to why SL2 is considered, the speaker points to the SL2(R) component containing hyperbolic structures, and later contrasts its noncompact Teichmuller behavior with the compact SU2 and p-adic settings.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker states that Teichmuller space lives in the SL2(R) character variety and relates it generally to hyperbolic structures, but does not compute or run a construction on that example.

passes.reason: Teichmüller space is the Fuchsian/maximal-Euler-class part of the real SL2 character variety, cited as motivation rather than as a failure case.
Teichmuller space is the maximal-Euler-class/discrete-faithful SL2(R) locus and is invoked as the noncompact discrete-action obstruction to density phenomena.
Teichmuller space inside the SL2(R) character variety consists of discrete faithful/maximal-Euler-class representations, an extremal component rather than a counterexample.

Teichmuller space is the discrete faithful, maximal-Euler-class part of the real SL2 character variety.

Teichmuller space is the maximal-Euler/discrete-faithful SL2(R) locus and is invoked as the noncompact real case where the mapping-class action is proper rather than densely chaotic.

examples.name: Markov triples from the once-punctured torus
examples.note: The punctured-torus boundary trace equation becomes the Markov-type equation when the boundary trace is fixed, illustrating the relative character variety and its integral-point/orbit questions.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker returns to the once-punctured torus, computes the boundary commutator trace equation in trace coordinates, and explains that fixing the trace gives the Markov triples equation.

passes.reason: Markov triples form the classical extremal Diophantine/worst-approximation solutions arising from the once-punctured-torus trace equation.
Markov triples are the parabolic boundary-trace solutions tied to worst Diophantine approximation, used here as an illustrative integral-points family.
Markov triples arise from the symmetric Markov equation at the parabolic once-punctured-torus trace and encode worst-case Diophantine approximation, hence an extremal family.

Markov triples form the classical extremal Diophantine configurations tied to worst rational approximation, and here they illustrate the once-punctured-torus relative character equation rather than refuting a claim.
Markov triples lie on the symmetric Markov equation and encode the classical extremal/worst cases in Diophantine approximation.
